sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go is an open-source programming language that focuses on simplicity, reliability, and efficiency, specifically designed to make it easy to build software at scale. Although it borrows ideas from existing languages, it has unusual properties that make effective Go programs different in character from programs written in its relatives. A straightforward trans

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This document gives tips for writing clear, idiomatic Go code. It augments the language specification , the Tour of Go , and How to Write Go Code , all of which you should read first.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This document gives tips for writing clear, idiomatic Go code. It augments the language specification , the Tour of Go , and How to Write Go Code , all of which you should read first.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Go package sources are intended to serve not only as the core library but also as examples of how to use the language. Moreover, many of the packages contain working, self-contained executable examples you can run directly from the go.dev web site, such as this one (if necessary, click on the word "Example" to open it up). If you have a question about ho

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Formatting issues are the most contentious but the least consequential. People can adapt to different formatting styles but it's better if they don't have to, and less time is devoted to the topic if everyone adheres to the same style. The problem is how to approach this Utopia without a long prescriptive style guide.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Formatting issues are the most contentious but the least consequential. People can adapt to different formatting styles but it's better if they don't have to, and less time is devoted to the topic if everyone adheres to

sourceRefs.fallbackText: With Go we take an unusual approach and let the machine take care of most formatting issues. The gofmt program (also available as go fmt , which operates at the package level rather than source file level) reads a Go program and emits the source in a standard style of indentation and vertical alignment, retaining and if necessary reformatting comments. If yo
sourceRefs.textQuote: With Go we take an unusual approach and let the machine take care of most formatting issues. The gofmt program (also available as go fmt , which operates at the package level rather than source file level) reads a Go pro

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As an example, there's no need to spend time lining up the comments on the fields of a structure. Gofmt will do that for you. Given the declaration
sourceRefs.textQuote: As an example, there's no need to spend time lining up the comments on the fields of a structure. Gofmt will do that for you. Given the declaration

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go provides C-style /* */ block comments and C++-style // line comments. Line comments are the norm; block comments appear mostly as package comments, but are useful within an expression or to disable large swaths of code.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go provides C-style /* */ block comments and C++-style // line comments. Line comments are the norm; block comments appear mostly as package comments, but are useful within an expression or to disable large swaths of cod

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Comments that appear before top-level declarations, with no intervening newlines, are considered to document the declaration itself. These “doc comments” are the primary documentation for a given Go package or command. For more about doc comments, see “ Go Doc Comments ”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Comments that appear before top-level declarations, with no intervening newlines, are considered to document the declaration itself. These “doc comments” are the primary documentation for a given Go package or command. F

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Names are as important in Go as in any other language. They even have semantic effect: the visibility of a name outside a package is determined by whether its first character is upper case. It's therefore worth spending a little time talking about naming conventions in Go programs.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Names are as important in Go as in any other language. They even have semantic effect: the visibility of a name outside a package is determined by whether its first character is upper case. It's therefore worth spending

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When a package is imported, the package name becomes an accessor for the contents. After
sourceRefs.textQuote: When a package is imported, the package name becomes an accessor for the contents. After

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the importing package can talk about bytes.Buffer . It's helpful if everyone using the package can use the same name to refer to its contents, which implies that the package name should be good: short, concise, evocative. By convention, packages are given lower case, single-word names; there should be no need for underscores or mixedCaps. Err on the side of
sourceRefs.textQuote: the importing package can talk about bytes.Buffer . It's helpful if everyone using the package can use the same name to refer to its contents, which implies that the package name should be good: short, concise, evocative

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another convention is that the package name is the base name of its source directory; the package in src/encoding/base64 is imported as "encoding/base64" but has name base64 , not encoding_base64 and not encodingBase64 .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another convention is that the package name is the base name of its source directory; the package in src/encoding/base64 is imported as "encoding/base64" but has name base64 , not encoding_base64 and not encodingBase64 .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The importer of a package will use the name to refer to its contents, so exported names in the package can use that fact to avoid repetition. (Don't use the import . notation, which can simplify tests that must run outside the package they are testing, but should otherwise be avoided.) For instance, the buffered reader type in the bufio package is called Rea

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another short example is once.Do ; once.Do(setup) reads well and would not be improved by writing once.DoOrWaitUntilDone(setup) . Long names don't automatically make things more readable. A helpful doc comment can often be more valuable than an extra long name.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another short example is once.Do ; once.Do(setup) reads well and would not be improved by writing once.DoOrWaitUntilDone(setup) . Long names don't automatically make things more readable. A helpful doc comment can often

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go doesn't provide automatic support for getters and setters. There's nothing wrong with providing getters and setters yourself, and it's often appropriate to do so, but it's neither idiomatic nor necessary to put Get into the getter's name. If you have a field called owner (lower case, unexported), the getter method should be called Owner (upper case, expor
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go doesn't provide automatic support for getters and setters. There's nothing wrong with providing getters and setters yourself, and it's often appropriate to do so, but it's neither idiomatic nor necessary to put Get in

sourceRefs.fallbackText: owner := obj.Owner() if owner != user { obj.SetOwner(user) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: owner := obj.Owner() if owner != user { obj.SetOwner(user) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: By convention, one-method interfaces are named by the method name plus an -er suffix or similar modification to construct an agent noun: Reader , Writer , Formatter , CloseNotifier etc.
sourceRefs.textQuote: By convention, one-method interfaces are named by the method name plus an -er suffix or similar modification to construct an agent noun: Reader , Writer , Formatter , CloseNotifier etc.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are a number of such names and it's productive to honor them and the function names they capture. Read , Write , Close , Flush , String and so on have canonical signatures and meanings. To avoid confusion, don't give your method one of those names unless it has the same signature and meaning. Conversely, if your type implements a method with the same m
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are a number of such names and it's productive to honor them and the function names they capture. Read , Write , Close , Flush , String and so on have canonical signatures and meanings. To avoid confusion, don't gi

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Finally, the convention in Go is to use MixedCaps or mixedCaps rather than underscores to write multiword names.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Finally, the convention in Go is to use MixedCaps or mixedCaps rather than underscores to write multiword names.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Like C, Go's formal grammar uses semicolons to terminate statements, but unlike in C, those semicolons do not appear in the source. Instead the lexer uses a simple rule to insert semicolons automatically as it scans, so the input text is mostly free of them.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Like C, Go's formal grammar uses semicolons to terminate statements, but unlike in C, those semicolons do not appear in the source. Instead the lexer uses a simple rule to insert semicolons automatically as it scans, so

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The rule is this. If the last token before a newline is an identifier (which includes words like int and float64 ), a basic literal such as a number or string constant, or one of the tokens
sourceRefs.textQuote: The rule is this. If the last token before a newline is an identifier (which includes words like int and float64 ), a basic literal such as a number or string constant, or one of the tokens

sourceRefs.fallbackText: break continue fallthrough return ++ -- ) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: break continue fallthrough return ++ -- ) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the lexer always inserts a semicolon after the token. This could be summarized as, “if the newline comes after a token that could end a statement, insert a semicolon”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: the lexer always inserts a semicolon after the token. This could be summarized as, “if the newline comes after a token that could end a statement, insert a semicolon”.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A semicolon can also be omitted immediately before a closing brace, so a statement such as
sourceRefs.textQuote: A semicolon can also be omitted immediately before a closing brace, so a statement such as

sourceRefs.fallbackText: go func() { for { dst <- <-src } }()
sourceRefs.textQuote: go func() { for { dst <- <-src } }()

sourceRefs.fallbackText: needs no semicolons. Idiomatic Go programs have semicolons only in places such as for loop clauses, to separate the initializer, condition, and continuation elements. They are also necessary to separate multiple statements on a line, should you write code that way.
sourceRefs.textQuote: needs no semicolons. Idiomatic Go programs have semicolons only in places such as for loop clauses, to separate the initializer, condition, and continuation elements. They are also necessary to separate multiple statemen

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One consequence of the semicolon insertion rules is that you cannot put the opening brace of a control structure ( if , for , switch , or select ) on the next line. If you do, a semicolon will be inserted before the brace, which could cause unwanted effects. Write them like this
sourceRefs.textQuote: One consequence of the semicolon insertion rules is that you cannot put the opening brace of a control structure ( if , for , switch , or select ) on the next line. If you do, a semicolon will be inserted before the brac

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if i < f() { g() }
sourceRefs.textQuote: if i < f() { g() }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if i < f() // wrong! { // wrong! g() }
sourceRefs.textQuote: if i < f() // wrong! { // wrong! g() }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The control structures of Go are related to those of C but differ in important ways. There is no do or while loop, only a slightly generalized for ; switch is more flexible; if and switch accept an optional initialization statement like that of for ; break and continue statements take an optional label to identify what to break or continue; and there are new
sourceRefs.textQuote: The control structures of Go are related to those of C but differ in important ways. There is no do or while loop, only a slightly generalized for ; switch is more flexible; if and switch accept an optional initializatio

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In Go a simple if looks like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: In Go a simple if looks like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if x > 0 { return y }
sourceRefs.textQuote: if x > 0 { return y }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Mandatory braces encourage writing simple if statements on multiple lines. It's good style to do so anyway, especially when the body contains a control statement such as a return or break .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Mandatory braces encourage writing simple if statements on multiple lines. It's good style to do so anyway, especially when the body contains a control statement such as a return or break .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Since if and switch accept an initialization statement, it's common to see one used to set up a local variable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Since if and switch accept an initialization statement, it's common to see one used to set up a local variable.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if err := file.Chmod(0664); err != nil { log.Print(err) return err }
sourceRefs.textQuote: if err := file.Chmod(0664); err != nil { log.Print(err) return err }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In the Go libraries, you'll find that when an if statement doesn't flow into the next statement—that is, the body ends in break , continue , goto , or return —the unnecessary else is omitted.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In the Go libraries, you'll find that when an if statement doesn't flow into the next statement—that is, the body ends in break , continue , goto , or return —the unnecessary else is omitted.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: f, err := os.Open(name) if err != nil { return err } codeUsing(f)
sourceRefs.textQuote: f, err := os.Open(name) if err != nil { return err } codeUsing(f)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is an example of a common situation where code must guard against a sequence of error conditions. The code reads well if the successful flow of control runs down the page, eliminating error cases as they arise. Since error cases tend to end in return statements, the resulting code needs no else statements.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This is an example of a common situation where code must guard against a sequence of error conditions. The code reads well if the successful flow of control runs down the page, eliminating error cases as they arise. Sinc

sourceRefs.fallbackText: f, err := os.Open(name) if err != nil { return err } d, err := f.Stat() if err != nil { f.Close() return err } codeUsing(f, d)
sourceRefs.textQuote: f, err := os.Open(name) if err != nil { return err } d, err := f.Stat() if err != nil { f.Close() return err } codeUsing(f, d)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An aside: The last example in the previous section demonstrates a detail of how the := short declaration form works. The declaration that calls os.Open reads,
sourceRefs.textQuote: An aside: The last example in the previous section demonstrates a detail of how the := short declaration form works. The declaration that calls os.Open reads,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: f, err := os.Open(name)
sourceRefs.textQuote: f, err := os.Open(name)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This statement declares two variables, f and err . A few lines later, the call to f.Stat reads,
sourceRefs.textQuote: This statement declares two variables, f and err . A few lines later, the call to f.Stat reads,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: d, err := f.Stat()
sourceRefs.textQuote: d, err := f.Stat()

sourceRefs.fallbackText: which looks as if it declares d and err . Notice, though, that err appears in both statements. This duplication is legal: err is declared by the first statement, but only re-assigned in the second. This means that the call to f.Stat uses the existing err variable declared above, and just gives it a new value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: which looks as if it declares d and err . Notice, though, that err appears in both statements. This duplication is legal: err is declared by the first statement, but only re-assigned in the second. This means that the ca

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In a := declaration a variable v may appear even if it has already been declared, provided:
sourceRefs.textQuote: In a := declaration a variable v may appear even if it has already been declared, provided:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: this declaration is in the same scope as the existing declaration of v (if v is already declared in an outer scope, the declaration will create a new variable §),
sourceRefs.textQuote: this declaration is in the same scope as the existing declaration of v (if v is already declared in an outer scope, the declaration will create a new variable §),

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the corresponding value in the initialization is assignable to v , and
sourceRefs.textQuote: the corresponding value in the initialization is assignable to v , and

sourceRefs.fallbackText: there is at least one other variable that is created by the declaration.
sourceRefs.textQuote: there is at least one other variable that is created by the declaration.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This unusual property is pure pragmatism, making it easy to use a single err value, for example, in a long if-else chain. You'll see it used often.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This unusual property is pure pragmatism, making it easy to use a single err value, for example, in a long if-else chain. You'll see it used often.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: § It's worth noting here that in Go the scope of function parameters and return values is the same as the function body, even though they appear lexically outside the braces that enclose the body.
sourceRefs.textQuote: § It's worth noting here that in Go the scope of function parameters and return values is the same as the function body, even though they appear lexically outside the braces that enclose the body.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Go for loop is similar to—but not the same as—C's. It unifies for and while and there is no do-while . There are three forms, only one of which has semicolons.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Go for loop is similar to—but not the same as—C's. It unifies for and while and there is no do-while . There are three forms, only one of which has semicolons.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // Like a C for for init; condition; post { } // Like a C while for condition { } // Like a C for(;;) for { }
sourceRefs.textQuote: // Like a C for for init; condition; post { } // Like a C while for condition { } // Like a C for(;;) for { }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Short declarations make it easy to declare the index variable right in the loop.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Short declarations make it easy to declare the index variable right in the loop.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: sum := 0 for i := 0; i < 10; i++ { sum += i }
sourceRefs.textQuote: sum := 0 for i := 0; i < 10; i++ { sum += i }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you're looping over an array, slice, string, or map, or reading from a channel, a range clause can manage the loop.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you're looping over an array, slice, string, or map, or reading from a channel, a range clause can manage the loop.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for key, value := range oldMap { newMap[key] = value }
sourceRefs.textQuote: for key, value := range oldMap { newMap[key] = value }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you only need the first item in the range (the key or index), drop the second:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you only need the first item in the range (the key or index), drop the second:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for key := range m { if key.expired() { delete(m, key) } }
sourceRefs.textQuote: for key := range m { if key.expired() { delete(m, key) } }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you only need the second item in the range (the value), use the blank identifier , an underscore, to discard the first:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you only need the second item in the range (the value), use the blank identifier , an underscore, to discard the first:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: sum := 0 for _, value := range array { sum += value }
sourceRefs.textQuote: sum := 0 for _, value := range array { sum += value }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The blank identifier has many uses, as described in a later section .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The blank identifier has many uses, as described in a later section .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For strings, the range does more work for you, breaking out individual Unicode code points by parsing the UTF-8. Erroneous encodings consume one byte and produce the replacement rune U+FFFD. (The name (with associated builtin type) rune is Go terminology for a single Unicode code point. See the language specification for details.) The loop

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for pos, char := range "日本\x80語" { // \x80 is an illegal UTF-8 encoding fmt.Printf("character %#U starts at byte position %d\n", char, pos) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: for pos, char := range "日本\x80語" { // \x80 is an illegal UTF-8 encoding fmt.Printf("character %#U starts at byte position %d\n", char, pos) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Finally, Go has no comma operator and ++ and -- are statements not expressions. Thus if you want to run multiple variables in a for you should use parallel assignment (although that precludes ++ and -- ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Finally, Go has no comma operator and ++ and -- are statements not expressions. Thus if you want to run multiple variables in a for you should use parallel assignment (although that precludes ++ and -- ).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // Reverse a for i, j := 0, len(a)-1; i < j; i, j = i+1, j-1 { a[i], a[j] = a[j], a[i] }
sourceRefs.textQuote: // Reverse a for i, j := 0, len(a)-1; i < j; i, j = i+1, j-1 { a[i], a[j] = a[j], a[i] }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go's switch is more general than C's. The expressions need not be constants or even integers, the cases are evaluated top to bottom until a match is found, and if the switch has no expression it switches on true . It's therefore possible—and idiomatic—to write an if - else - if - else chain as a switch .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go's switch is more general than C's. The expressions need not be constants or even integers, the cases are evaluated top to bottom until a match is found, and if the switch has no expression it switches on true . It's t

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func unhex(c byte) byte { switch { case '0' <= c && c <= '9': return c - '0' case 'a' <= c && c <= 'f': return c - 'a' + 10 case 'A' <= c && c <= 'F': return c - 'A' + 10 } return 0 }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func unhex(c byte) byte { switch { case '0' <= c && c <= '9': return c - '0' case 'a' <= c && c <= 'f': return c - 'a' + 10 case 'A' <= c && c <= 'F': return c - 'A' + 10 } return 0 }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is no automatic fall through, but cases can be presented in comma-separated lists.
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is no automatic fall through, but cases can be presented in comma-separated lists.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func shouldEscape(c byte) bool { switch c { case ' ', '?', '&', '=', '#', '+', '%': return true } return false }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func shouldEscape(c byte) bool { switch c { case ' ', '?', '&', '=', '#', '+', '%': return true } return false }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Although they are not nearly as common in Go as some other C-like languages, break statements can be used to terminate a switch early. Sometimes, though, it's necessary to break out of a surrounding loop, not the switch, and in Go that can be accomplished by putting a label on the loop and "breaking" to that label. This example shows both uses.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Of course, the continue statement also accepts an optional label but it applies only to loops.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Of course, the continue statement also accepts an optional label but it applies only to loops.

sourceRefs.textQuote: To close this section, here's a comparison routine for byte slices that uses two switch statements:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // Compare returns an integer comparing the two byte slices, // lexicographically. // The result will be 0 if a == b, -1 if a < b, and +1 if a > b func Compare(a, b []byte) int { for i := 0; i < len(a) && i < len(b); i++ { switch { case a[i] > b[i]: return 1 case a[i] < b[i]: return -1 } } switch { case len(a) > len(b): return 1 case len(a) < len(b): return

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A switch can also be used to discover the dynamic type of an interface variable. Such a type switch uses the syntax of a type assertion with the keyword type inside the parentheses. sourceRefs.fallbackText: One of Go's unusual features is that functions and methods can return multiple values. This form can be used to improve on a couple of clumsy idioms in C programs: in-band error returns such as -1 for EOF and modifying an argument passed by address.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In C, a write error is signaled by a negative count with the error code secreted away in a volatile location. In Go, Write can return a count and an error: “Yes, you wrote some bytes but not all of them because you filled the device”. The signature of the Write method on files from package os is:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A similar approach obviates the need to pass a pointer to a return value to simulate a reference parameter. Here's a simple-minded function to grab a number from a position in a byte slice, returning the number and the next position.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A similar approach obviates the need to pass a pointer to a return value to simulate a reference parameter. Here's a simple-minded function to grab a number from a position in a byte slice, returning the number and the n

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func nextInt(b []byte, i int) (int, int) { for ; i < len(b) && !isDigit(b[i]); i++ { } x := 0 for ; i < len(b) && isDigit(b[i]); i++ { x = x*10 + int(b[i]) - '0' } return x, i }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func nextInt(b []byte, i int) (int, int) { for ; i < len(b) && !isDigit(b[i]); i++ { } x := 0 for ; i < len(b) && isDigit(b[i]); i++ { x = x*10 + int(b[i]) - '0' } return x, i }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The return or result "parameters" of a Go function can be given names and used as regular variables, just like the incoming parameters. When named, they are initialized to the zero values for their types when the function begins; if the function executes a return statement with no arguments, the current values of the result parameters are used as the returne

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The names are not mandatory but they can make code shorter and clearer: they're documentation. If we name the results of nextInt it becomes obvious which returned int is which.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Because named results are initialized and tied to an unadorned return, they can simplify as well as clarify. Here's a version of io.ReadFull that uses them well:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Because named results are initialized and tied to an unadorned return, they can simplify as well as clarify. Here's a version of io.ReadFull that uses them well:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: func ReadFull(r Reader, buf []byte) (n int, err error) { for len(buf) > 0 && err == nil { var nr int nr, err = r.Read(buf) n += nr buf = buf[nr:] } return }
sourceRefs.textQuote: func ReadFull(r Reader, buf []byte) (n int, err error) { for len(buf) > 0 && err == nil { var nr int nr, err = r.Read(buf) n += nr buf = buf[nr:] } return }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go's defer statement schedules a function call (the deferred function) to be run immediately before the function executing the defer returns. It's an unusual but effective way to deal with situations such as resources that must be released regardless of which path a function takes to return. The canonical examples are unlocking a mutex or closing a file.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go's defer statement schedules a function call (the deferred function) to be run immediately before the function executing the defer returns. It's an unusual but effective way to deal with situations such as resources th

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // Contents returns the file's contents as a string. func Contents(filename string) (string, error) { f, err := os.Open(filename) if err != nil { return "", err } defer f.Close() // f.Close will run when we're finished. var result []byte buf := make([]byte, 100) for { n, err := f.Read(buf[0:]) result = append(result, buf[0:n]...) // append is discussed later
sourceRefs.textQuote: // Contents returns the file's contents as a string. func Contents(filename string) (string, error) { f, err := os.Open(filename) if err != nil { return "", err } defer f.Close() // f.Close will run when we're finished.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Deferring a call to a function such as Close has two advantages. First, it guarantees that you will never forget to close the file, a mistake that's easy to make if you later edit the function to add a new return path. Second, it means that the close sits near the open, which is much clearer than placing it at the end of the function.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Deferring a call to a function such as Close has two advantages. First, it guarantees that you will never forget to close the file, a mistake that's easy to make if you later edit the function to add a new return path. S

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The arguments to the deferred function (which include the receiver if the function is a method) are evaluated when the defer executes, not when the call executes. Besides avoiding worries about variables changing values as the function executes, this means that a single deferred call site can defer multiple function executions. Here's a silly example.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The arguments to the deferred function (which include the receiver if the function is a method) are evaluated when the defer executes, not when the call executes. Besides avoiding worries about variables changing values

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for i := 0; i < 5; i++ { defer fmt.Printf("%d ", i) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: for i := 0; i < 5; i++ { defer fmt.Printf("%d ", i) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Deferred functions are executed in LIFO order, so this code will cause 4 3 2 1 0 to be printed when the function returns. A more plausible example is a simple way to trace function execution through the program. We could write a couple of simple tracing routines like this:
